title: Psalms 95
subtitle: Come, Let Us Sing for Joy to the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–7a — “He Is the Great God, the Great King”

heb.gloss: let us shout / let us cry out
heb.paragraph: נָרִיעָה (nārîʿâ) = let us shout joyfully/triumphantly (specifically a shout of joy or triumph, not just any crying out)

notes.note: "Come, let us sing for joy to the LORD." The triple invitation: sing, shout, come with thanksgiving. Worship is active, loud, and communal.

"The LORD is the great God, the great King above all gods. In his hand are the depths of the earth; the sea is his, for he made it." Total ownership: depths, heights, sea, land. All created, all held.

"Come, let us bow down in worship, let us kneel before the LORD our Maker, for he is our God and we are the people of his pasture." Posture matches theology: the great King deserves kneeling. The shepherd owns the flock.

refs.note: Hebrews quotes verses 7-11 extensively to warn against hardening hearts as Israel did.
God setting a certain day called 'Today' through David emphasizes the psalm's urgency.
The repeated 'today if you hear his voice' becomes a key New Testament exhortation.

hist.context: Psalm 95 combines joyful praise (vv.1-7a) with prophetic warning (vv.7b-11). The call to worship 'the rock of our salvation' leads to acknowledgment of God as Creator and Shepherd. The sudden shift — 'today, if only you would hear his voice' — introduces divine speech warning against rebellion. This psalm traditionally opened Jewish morning prayer.

sections.header: Verses 8–11 — "Today, If Only You Would Hear His Voice"

heb.paragraph: "Today (hayyōm), if only you would hear his voice, ‘Do not harden your hearts as you did at Meribah, as you did that day at Massah in the wilderness’" (vv.7b–8). The psalm’s dramatic turn: from joyful worship to urgent warning. Hebrews 3:7–11 quotes this extensively: "Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts." The wilderness generation tested God for forty years (v.9); God was angry and swore: "They shall never enter my rest" (v.11). The warning: worship without obedience leads nowhere. "Today" is urgent — there is a deadline.

notes.note: "Today, if only you would hear his voice, do not harden your hearts." The most pastorally urgent verse in the Psalter. Hebrews makes it the key to the new covenant warning: TODAY. Not tomorrow. Not eventually.

"For forty years I was angry with that generation. I said, ‘They are a people whose hearts go astray, and they have not known my ways.’" Forty years of testing; forty years of anger. They saw the works but missed the ways.

"‘So I declared on oath in my anger: They shall never enter my rest.’" The irrevocable consequence: exclusion from rest. Hebrews 3–4 develops this into a theology of entering God’s rest through faith.

refs.note: Exodus 17:1-7 records Meribah and Massah where Israel tested God, but the oath about not entering rest refers to the broader wilderness rebellion (Num 14:20-35)
God's oath that the rebellious generation would not see the promised land fulfills the psalm's warning.
The promise of entering rest still standing warns the psalm's 'rest' remains relevant.

hist.context: The divine oath — 'they shall never enter my rest' (v.11) — barred the exodus generation from Canaan. Hebrews interprets this 'rest' as still available: a sabbath rest that remains for God's people. The psalm thus becomes paradigmatic: every generation faces the 'today' of faith or rebellion.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "The LORD is the great God, the great King above all gods…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVLet us come before him with thanksgiving and extol him with music and song.ESVLet us come into his presence with thanksgiving; let us make a joyful noise to him with songs of praise!NIV“Do not harden your hearts as you did at Meribah, as you did that day at Massah in the wilderness,ESVdo not harden your hearts, as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness,

rec.text: ""Come, let us sing for joy to the LORD." The triple invitation: sing, shout, come with thanksgiving. Worship is active, loud, and communal."

rows.label: Today — 95:7b–11
rows.text: "TODAY, if you hear his voice, do not HARDEN your hearts." Meribah. "They shall never enter my rest."

hebtext.gloss: let us shout / let us cry out
hebtext.note: In v.1, 'let us sing for joy' is נְרַנְּנָה (nerannenah), while נָרִיעָה (nārîʿâ) means 'let us shout joyfully'

"Today (hayyōm), if only you would hear his voice, ‘Do not harden your hearts as you did at Meribah, as you did that day at Massah in the wilderness’" (vv.7b–8). The psalm’s dramatic turn: from joyful worship to urgent warning. Hebrews 3:7–11 quotes this extensively: "Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts." The wilderness generation tested God for forty years (v.9); God was angry and swore: "They shall never enter my rest" (v.11). The warning: worship without obedience leads nowhere. "Today" is urgent — there is a deadline.

themes.note: Worship peaks: the venite — monastic call to praise. Prophecy peaks: Hebrews 3–4 quotes extensively. Judgment: exclusion from rest. The psalm splits: joy (vv.1–7a) then warning (vv.7b–11).

prayer_prompt: The psalm invites worship but warns against hardening hearts as Israel did. Consider asking God about the hardening in your own heart — what softening He wants to do.
